coredata.dc:title: Constructing a novel carbon skeleton to anchor Sn/SnO2 nanodots for flexible supercapacitor with excellent rate capability
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhen
Zhang, Chenying
Bu, Jingting
Zhang, Long
Cheng, Lingli
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: Designing SnO2/carbon composites is an effective strategy to improve the conductivity and buffer the volume expansion of SnO2. However, it remains a challenge to combine SnO2 and carbon materials tightly as a stable integration. Herein, a facile and versatile strategy of Sn/SnO2 nanodots anchored tightly into carbon nanofibers (CNFs) with the decoration of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for high performance supercapacitor is reported. Through a simple electrospinning and carbonization reduction process, a novel multidimensional carbon skeleton of GQD/CNF effectively improves the conductivity, and importantly, abundant Sn–O–C covalent bonds are constructed to anchor SnO2 nanodots tightly into GQD/CNF, suppressing SnO2 aggregation and facilitating electron/ion transfer kinetics. Consequently, as self-supporting and binder-free electrode material, Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF displays high specific capacitance of 168.6 mA h g−1 (1349 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate capability (88.9% retention at 20 A g−1). Furthermore, a flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor based on Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF and GQD/CNF achieves a high energy density of 32.3 W h kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1 with remarkable flexibility and cycling stability (86.1% retention after 5000 cycles). The excellent electrochemical performances demonstrate that this novel carbon skeleton anchored active materials shows great potential for electrochemical energy storage applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF, Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2 composites
Assembly of flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Consortium national de formation en santé, Volet Université d'Ottawa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: In this work, we report a highly designable strategy for the synthesis of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF composite by co-electrospinning of tin salt and GQDs through a simple electrospinning and carbonization reduction process. The strong Sn–O–C covalent bonds between SnO2 nanodots and CNFs or GQDs makes SnO2 nanodots tightly anchor into the conductive carbon skeleton of GQD/CNF, which is conducive to the enhanced dynamic characteristics and interfacial stability. In this composite, CNFs serve as a conductive and freestanding carbon skeleton for rapid electron transfer to improve the conductivity. SnO2 nanodots distributed uniformly in a multidimensional carbon skeleton fully contribute their high pseudocapacitance activity. Meanwhile, Sn nanodots produce more active sites and more efficient diffusion pathways for electron transfer. GQDs not only improve the conductivity, but also enhance the mechanical strength of CNFs, and increase the number of Sn–O–C covalent bonds. The self-supporting and binder-free electrode based on Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF displays high specific capacitance of 168.6 mA h g−1 (1349 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate capability (88.9% retention rate at 20 A g−1). For the practical application, a flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor configuration with GQD/CNF as the negative electrode and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF as the positive electrode exhibits high energy and power density and excellent cycle stability.This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11975148, 11775138), and Shanghai Industrial Collaborative Innovation Project (No. 2021-cyxt1-kj46).
This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11975148 , 11775138 ), and Shanghai Industrial Collaborative Innovation Project (No. 2021-cyxt1-kj46 ).

(e) Rate capability, (f) EIS (inset: the equivalent electrical circuit and the EIS in the region of high frequency), (g) Ragone plots of the device (comparison with reported asymmetric supercapacitors based on SnO2/carbon composites), (h) CV curves of the device under different bending conditions at 20 mV s−1 (inset: the digital photograph of the bending device), and (i) Cycling test of the device after 5000 cycles at 5 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Constructing a novel carbon skeleton to anchor Sn/SnO2 nanodots for flexible supercapacitor with excellent rate capability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing SnO2/carbon composites is an effective strategy to improve the conductivity and buffer the volume expansion of SnO2. However, it remains a challenge to combine SnO2 and carbon materials tightly as a stable integration. Herein, a facile and versatile strategy of Sn/SnO2 nanodots anchored tightly into carbon nanofibers (CNFs) with the decoration of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for high performance supercapacitor is reported. Through a simple electrospinning and carbonization reduction process, a novel multidimensional carbon skeleton of GQD/CNF effectively improves the conductivity, and importantly, abundant Sn–O–C covalent bonds are constructed to anchor SnO2 nanodots tightly into GQD/CNF, suppressing SnO2 aggregation and facilitating electron/ion transfer kinetics. Consequently, as self-supporting and binder-free electrode material, Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF displays high specific capacitance of 168.6 mA h g−1 (1349 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate capability (88.9% retention at 20 A g−1). Furthermore, a flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor based on Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF and GQD/CNF achieves a high energy density of 32.3 W h kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1 with remarkable flexibility and cycling stability (86.1% retention after 5000 cycles). The excellent electrochemical performances demonstrate that this novel carbon skeleton anchored active materials shows great potential for electrochemical energy storage applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the exhaustion of fossil fuels and other forms of non-renewable energy resources, clean energy and renewable energy have received more and more attention. Among various kinds of electrochemical energy storage devices, supercapacitors (SCs) have many advantages such as fast charge and discharge, high power density and long cyclic life [1,2]. However, compared to batteries, the lower energy density of SCs impedes their further development [3]. The structure and composition of electrode materials are the key factors of SCs. Therefore, scientists now focus on developing new electrode materials to improve the performance of SCs [4].

Tin-based materials (Sn, SnO2, SnS2, etc.) have been widely used as electrode materials for lithium ion batteries and SCs due to their high theoretical capacity, abundant reserves and environmental friendliness [5]. As an n-type pseudocapacitive metal oxide, SnO2 has high theoretical capacity of 1494 mA h g−1. However, during the charge-discharge cycle, the low conductivity and the volume expansion lead to poor electrical contact between the collector and the SnO2 electrode, which affects the charge transfer and the cyclic stability of the electrode, and limits the application of SnO2 in electrochemical energy storage systems [6–8]. In order to address the problem, many researches are focused on reducing the size of SnO2 to alleviate the volume expansion, but the particle aggregation of nano-structured SnO2 is observed after several charge/discharge cycles [9,10]. Another strategy is toward designing SnO2/carbon-based composites to buffer the volume expansion and improve the conductivity due to excellent properties of carbon-based materials such as chemical stability, good electrical conductivity and multi-dimensional structure [11]. Kim et al. reported the intermixed reduced graphene oxide flakes and SnO2 nanoparticles to enhance the charge transport and improve the electrochemical performance [12]. Velmurugan et al. synthesized SnO2/graphene nanocomposites with a specific capacitance of 818.6 F g−1 and a capacitance retention of 95.1% after 1000 cycles [13]. Rani et al. synthesized mesoporous SnO2@C composite, which exhibited a specific capacitance of 432 F g−1 with 95.5% of initial capacitance retained after 2000 cycles [10].

However, among these prepared SnO2/carbon-based composites, most of the SnO2 nanoparticles are distributed on the surface of the carbon matrix materials and cannot be tightly bound to the carbon material, thus easily leading to the aggregation of SnO2 and dropping off from carbon skeleton during charge-discharge cycles [14,15]. In this situation, constructing of Sn–O–C covalent bonds can enhance the interfacial interaction between SnO2 nanoparticles and carbon materials (such as carbon nanofibers, graphene, porous carbon and graphene quantum dots), which is an effective way to anchor SnO2 tightly into the carbon-based skeleton [16–20].

In this work, we report a highly designable strategy for the synthesis of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF composite by co-electrospinning of tin salt and GQDs through a simple electrospinning and carbonization reduction process. The strong Sn–O–C covalent bonds between SnO2 nanodots and CNFs or GQDs makes SnO2 nanodots tightly anchor into the conductive carbon skeleton of GQD/CNF, which is conducive to the enhanced dynamic characteristics and interfacial stability. In this composite, CNFs serve as a conductive and freestanding carbon skeleton for rapid electron transfer to improve the conductivity. SnO2 nanodots distributed uniformly in a multidimensional carbon skeleton fully contribute their high pseudocapacitance activity. Meanwhile, Sn nanodots produce more active sites and more efficient diffusion pathways for electron transfer. GQDs not only improve the conductivity, but also enhance the mechanical strength of CNFs, and increase the number of Sn–O–C covalent bonds. The self-supporting and binder-free electrode based on Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF displays high specific capacitance of 168.6 mA h g−1 (1349 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate capability (88.9% retention rate at 20 A g−1). For the practical application, a flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor configuration with GQD/CNF as the negative electrode and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF as the positive electrode exhibits high energy and power density and excellent cycle stability.

Stannous chloride dihydrate (SnCl2·2H2O), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), potassium hydroxide (KOH), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) was purchased from Sigma-Aldric Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Bituminous coal powder was purchased from Taian Ford New Material Co., Ltd. All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used directly without additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF, Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The coal-based GQDs were prepared following a chemical oxidation method reported by Zhang et al. [21]. First, 0.6 g PAN was dissolved in 6 mL DMF solvent to form a homogeneous solution. Then 0.5 g SnCl2·2H2O and 0.3 g GQDs were added into the above solution and the precursor solution was obtained by stirring for 24 h at room temperature. Afterwards, the precursor solution was loaded into a 10 mL syringe of the electrospinning setup with the distance between the needle and the receiver of 15 cm and the constant voltage of 15 kV at a flow rate of 0.14 mm min−1. After spinning, the SnCl2/GQD/PAN nanofibers were obtained on the aluminum foil followed by solvent evaporation at 60 °C for 3 h. Next, the SnCl2/GQD/PAN nanofibers were pre-oxidized in a muffle furnace at 300 °C for 1 h, and finally annealed in N2 atmosphere at 500 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 in a tubular furnace to obtain the Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF positive electrode material.

As a comparison sample, Sn/SnO2/CNF was obtained under the same experimental conditions only without adding GQDs into the precursor solution. In order to investigate the role of the GQD/CNF carbon skeleton, Sn/SnO2 was prepared following a hydrothermal method reported by Wang et al. [22].

0.3 g GQDs and 0.6 g PAN were dissolved in 6 mL DMF solution to form a precursor solution. GQD/PAN nanofibers were obtained under the same experimental conditions of spinning. After pre-oxidized at 280 °C for 1 h and annealed at 500 °C for 2 h, GQD/CNF negative electrode material was prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: The flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor was assembled using Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF as the battery-type positive electrode material and GQD/CNF as the capacitive-type negative electrode material. Both positive and negative electrodes are flexible, self-supporting and additive-free. To obtain the PVA/KOH gel electrolyte, 6 g PVA was dissolved into 50 mL deionized water and stirred in an oil bath at 85 °C until a transparent PVA solution was formed. Then, 6 g KOH was dissolved into 20 mL deionized water and added into the above PVA solution. PVA/KOH gel has been widely used as alkaline solid polymer electrolyte for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors in recent years for its hydrophilic property and high ionic conductivity [23,24]. The Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF positive electrode and Cu wire were pressed directly on the PET film (the same treatment for GQD/CNF negative electrode), making sure that the electrodes were fully in contact with the PVA/KOH gel electrolyte. Finally, the two electrodes were assembled together to obtain the flexible solid-state asymmetric Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF//GQD/CNF supercapacitor.

The morphology and structure of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF were characterized by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7500 F, Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010F. Japan). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data was collected on an X-ray Diffraction (Rigaku/MAX-220, Shimadzu, Japan) using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) over the 2θ range from 10° to 80° with the scanning rate of 8°min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was collected by using Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer.

Electrochemical performance tests of the positive and negative electrodes including CV, GCD, and EIS were performed through electrochemical work station (Autolab PGSTAT128 N). The single electrode was measured in a three-electrode system. Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF or GQD/CNF electrode was acted as the working electrode, while Hg/HgO electrode (1 M KOH), platinum line and 1 M KOH solution were used to test electrochemical performance. The mass loading of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF and GQD/CNF electrode were 1.1 and 1.5 mg cm−2 respectively.

The specific capacitance (mA h g−1) of the positive electrode was calculated from the GCD curves according to the following formula (1):

The synthesis routes of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF and GQD/CNF are schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . First, SnCl2·2H2O, GQDs and PAN were dissolved in DMF solvent, and SnCl2/GQD/PAN nanofiber membrane was formed by electrospinning. Then after the pre-oxidation treatment in air, SnCl2 was oxidized to SnO2. Finally, PAN was carbonized through high temperature carbonization in N2 atmosphere. Meanwhile, SnO2 was partially reduced to metal Sn by carbon in oxygen free atmosphere, as shown in formula (7), and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF composite was obtained [16].
(7) SnO2 + C → Sn + CO2

The preparation method of negative electrode was similar to that of positive electrode. GQDs and PAN were dissolved into DMF solution, and GQD/CNF was obtained by the pre-oxidation in air and carbonization in N2 atmosphere. Finally, the Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF//GQD/CNF flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor was assembled by using a battery behavior of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF as positive electrode and a capacitive behavior of GQD/CNF as negative electrode with PVA/KOH gel electrolyte.

Designing SnO2/carbon-based composites is an effective strategy to improve the conductivity and buffer the volume expansion of SnO2 material. However, it remains a challenge to combine SnO2 and carbon materials tightly as a stable integration with the lower internal resistance. To solve this problem, we proposed a highly designable strategy for fabricating Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF composite through a simple multicomponent electrospinning and carbonization reduction process. Carbon nanofibers embedded with graphene quantum dots (GQD/CNF) as a multidimensional carbon skeleton not only improve the conductivity and buffer volume expansion of SnO2, more importantly, but also provide abundant Sn–O–C covalent bonds to enhance the interfacial interaction between SnO2 and carbon materials, which anchors SnO2 tightly into GQD/CNF carbon skeleton to be a stable integration. SnO2 nanodots are distributed uniformly and contribute their high capacitance activity. The decoration of metal Sn reduces the intrinsic resistance of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF, provides more active sites for faradaic reaction, and upgrades the electrical conductivity of the composite to enhance the specific capacitance behavior of SnO2 [25–27]. In conclusion, it can be predicted that the designed Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF electrode material has high capacitance, remarkable rate capability and cyclic stability.

SEM and TEM were employed to observe the morphology and microstructure of the samples. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the pure CNFs derived from PAN have a smooth surface with a diameter of 220–250 nm. From Fig. 2c and d, it can be clearly observed that numerous nanoparticles are distributed on the surface of Sn/SnO2/CNF derived from Sn salt and PAN. Compared to the above two kinds of nanofibers, when GQDs are added to the electrospinning precursor, the morphology of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF nanofiber is similar to that of Sn/SnO2/CNF nanofibers as shown in Fig. 2e and f. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. S1 that pure CNFs membrane are fragile, while GQD/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF membrane maintain good mechanical stability after bending. It demonstrates that the addition of GQDs can improve the mechanical strength and the integrity of the nanofibers, which is of great significance for the practical application of nanofibers in flexible devices. The elemental mapping analysis (Fig. 2h–k) corresponding to the SEM image (Fig. 2g) shows the uniform distribution of C, N, O and Sn elements.

TEM images of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF are shown in Fig. 3 a–c, and it can be seen that there are many nanoparticles distributed in the carbon nanofibers with the sizes ranging from several nanometers to tens of nanometers. Fig. 3d shows the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the composite. The lattice fringe spacings of 0.29 nm, 0.28 nm are attributed to the (200) and (101) planes of Sn [26,28,29], while 0.26 nm corresponds to (101) plane of SnO2 [16] and 0.21 nm to (100) plane of GQDs [21]. The particle sizes of SnO2 nanodots, Sn nanodots and GQDs are about 15 nm, 5 nm and 3 nm, respectively.

The composition and phase structures of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF were analyzed by XRD measurement (Fig. 4 a). The board diffraction peak at 2θ of about 25° is corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite. The diffraction peaks of 26.5° (110), 33.8° (101), 37.9° (200), 51.7° (211) and 65.8° (301) can be clearly indexed to tetragonal phase SnO2 (JCPDS No.77-0450) [30], while 30° (200), 32° (101), 43° (220), 44° (211), 55° (301), 62° (112), 63° (400), 64° (321), 72° (420), 73° (411), and 79° (312) peaks can be assigned to β-metallic Sn with a tetragonal structure (JCPDS No.04-0673) [31].

The elemental compositions and the valence states in Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF were investigated by XPS measurement. From the XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4b), the content of C, O, N and Sn elements in Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF are displayed in Table S1. High-resolution XPS C 1s, as shown in Fig. 4c and Fig. S2a, both are fitted into three peaks, corresponding to CC/C–C (284.6 eV), Sn–O–C (286.2 eV), and CO (288.4 eV) [32,33]. The N 1s spectrum fitted into four peaks (Fig. 4d and Fig. S2b) at 398.1 eV, 399.9 eV, 401.2 eV and 403.5 eV are attributed to the pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and oxidized N respectively, and the heteroatom N can increase the freedom electronics and provide more active sites [19,34,35]. Two symmetric peaks located at the binding energies of 495.9 and 487.4 eV (Fig. 4e and Fig. S2c) are assigned to Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 for SnO2, while the peaks centered at 485.9 eV and 493.9 eV for Sn0 [36,37]. In Fig. 4f and Fig. S2d, the O 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks, which are assigned to the binding energy of Sn–O (531.2 eV), Sn–O–C (532.4 eV), and C–O/OC–OH (533.8 eV) [17,38]. The contents of oxygen-containing functional groups of the two samples are calculated as shown in Table 1 .

It can be clearly seen that there are more Sn–O–C covalent bonds in Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF (56%) than that of in Sn/SnO2/CNF (40%), indicating that GQDs facilitate the formation of more Sn–O–C covalent bonds. Abundant Sn–O–C covalent bonds enhance the interfacial interaction between SnO2 and carbon materials, and anchor SnO2 tightly into GQD/CNF carbon skeleton to be a stable integration, which reduces the intrinsic resistance and improves the electrical conductivity of the composite, and efficiently suppresses the aggregation of SnO2 nanodots. Optimized structure and electron density distributions calculated by DFT methods, electron transfer path in the p-n heterojunction structure have been investigated to further understand the correlation between the electrochemical performance and the interfacial interaction between SnO2 and GQDs [20,39,40], which theoretically explains the mechanism that Sn–O–C covalent bond is beneficial to charge transfer and structure stability.

The electrochemical properties of Sn/SnO2, Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF were investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a three-electrode system with 1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. Fig. S3 shows that the performance of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF composite is optimized under an appropriate condition (the mass ratio of SnCl2: GQDs in the precursor: 2:1, carbonization temperature: 500 °C). Fig. 5 a shows the CV curves of the samples at the scan rate of 20 mV s−1 within the potential window of 0–0.5 V. The typical peaks in the CV curves reveal the existence of reversible faradaic reactions. The corresponding redox reactions can be described as: (1) SnO2 + M+ + e−
↔
SnOOM (where M+ is the proton or electrolyte ion); (2) Sn
↔
Sn(OH)6 2- (IV) [26]. The CV curve of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF shows the largest area swept, demonstrating the robust faradaic behavior as battery-type electrode materials. The nonlinear nature of GCD curves (Fig. 5b) further confirms the faradaic capacitance behavior. Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF electrode shows a high specific capacitance of 168.6 mA h g−1 (1349 F g−1) at 1 A g−1, which is much higher than Sn/SnO2 with 93.3 mA h g−1 (746 F g−1), Sn/SnO2/CNF with 130.8 mA h g−1 (1046 F g−1) and previously reported SnO2-based electrode materials, such as Sn/SnO2@C (107.8 mA h g−1) [26], SnO2/CNF (590 F g−1) [35], SnO2@ NGO (387 F g−1) [41]. Moreover, the voltage window of the Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF increases from 0-0.4 V to 0–0.45 V, which may be explained that the p-n heterojunction constructed by GQDs (p-type) and SnO2 (n-type) plays an electronic synergistic role to widen the potential window and promote an electron and ion conduction between the electrolyte and the active materials [42].

Fig. 5c shows the shapes of CV curves for Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF electrode have no visible change even at a high scan rate of 200 mV s−1, indicating the electrolyte ion can efficiently transport throughout the electrode. Fig. 5d displays the GCD curves of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF electrode at different current densities. An apparent potential plateau without obvious voltage drop can be observed, which is consistent with the redox peaks observed in CV curves, implying that it is a kind of battery-type electrode material with typical electrochemical behaviors. Fig. 5e shows the specific capacitance and the capacitance retention of Sn/SnO2, Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF electrodes at different current density from 1 to 20 A g−1. About 88.9% of the specific capacitance is retained for the Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF electrode from 1 to 20 A g−1, much higher than that for Sn/SnO2 (53.9%) and Sn/SnO2/CNF (65.8%), implying its higher rate capability, superior fast charging and discharging capacity and better electrical conductivity. Comparison of the electrochemical characteristics of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF with reported SnO2/carbon-based electrode materials is presented in Table 2 .

Compared to other SnO2/carbon-based electrode materials, the prepared Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF exhibits ultra-high capacitance and outstanding rate capability, which attributes to the improved electrical conductivity of GQD/CNF carbon skeleton, the increased active sites of SnO2, and the reduced intrinsic resistance by the strong force of Sn–O–C covalent bonds. EIS measurements was used to evaluate the transport of electrons and electrolyte ions through the electrodes. Fig. 5f displays the Nyquist plot of the Sn/SnO2, Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF electrodes in the frequency range from 100 to 0.01 KHz at open circuit potential. The series resistance (R s) including electrolyte resistance, electrode resistance, and contact resistance is represented by the intercept of the semicircle on the real axis in the high frequency region [47]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 5f and Table S2, the R s of Sn/SnO2, Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF electrodes are 0.51, 0.35, and 0.12 Ω, respectively, suggesting that high conductivity GQD/CNF establishes electron transport pathways, and more importantly, Sn–O–C covalent bonds enhance the interfacial interaction between SnO2 and carbon materials, which effectively reduces electrolyte resistance, electrode resistance, and contact resistance. The charge-transfer resistance (R ct, diameter of the semicircle) and Warburg resistance (R w, projection distance of Warburg area on the real axis) of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF are also much lower than that of Sn/SnO2 and Sn/SnO2/CNF electrodes, conducive to electrolyte ions and electron transfer and diffusion [48,49].

Kinetic analysis was performed to understand the charge storage mechanism by recording CV curves of the Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF at different scan rates (Fig. 5c and Fig. S4a). The relationship between the peak current (i) and sweep rate (v) can be expressed using the power-law relationship [50–52]:
(8)
i = a
v b

The b value provides information about the charge storage mechanism. For a redox reaction caused by semi-infinite diffusion, such as battery-type material, b value is about 0.5. While for a capacitive process, such as capacitor-type material, b value is about 1. The calculated b-values of Sn/SnO2/CNF and Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF are 0.55 and 0.63 (Fig. 6 a), indicating of mainly diffusion-controlled energy storage mechanism for both electrode materials. With the addition of GQDs, the b value increases, indicative of more capacitive energy storage behavior. Quantitatively, the definite capacitive contribution to the total capacity can be determined according to the following formula [52]:
(9)
i
( V )
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
Where k 1 v, k 2 v 1/2 stand for the capacitive reaction and diffusion reaction respectively. By determining the values of k 1 and k 2, the capacitive contribution and the diffusion contribution can be distinguished [53]. Fig. 6b demonstrates an 87% capacitive contribution of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF from the total capacity at 200 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 6c, the surface capacitive contributions increase from 25% to 87% with the increase of the scan rate from 10 to 200 mV s−1. For comparison, the corresponding values of Sn/SnO2/CNF at each scan rate are shown in Fig. 6d and Fig. S4b. It can be seen that the capacitive contributions of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF are higher than that of Sn/SnO2/CNF at the same scanning rate, indicating that GQDs endow the electrode with fast electrochemical reaction kinetics, which is conducive to high rate performance [34].

To fulfill the needs of flexibility, portability and stability in practical applications, a flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor was assembled with Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF as positive electrode and GQD/CNF as negative electrode using PVA-KOH gel as the electrolyte. GQD/CNF was selected as the negative electrode material due to its high capacitive-type performance of 255 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 (Fig. S5). To optimize the electrochemical performance of the device, the mass of 1 mg Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF and 3.2 mg GQD/CNF were used to maintain the charge balance. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the positive and negative electrodes have the potential windows for Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF (0–0.45 V) and GQD/CNF (−1 to 0 V), and the potential window of flexible asymmetric Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF//GQD/CNF supercapacitor can reach 1.6 V (Fig. 7b). Fig. 7c shows the shape of the CV curves remain unchanged even at high scan rate (100 mV s−1), revealing the rapid charge-discharge capability of the device. The device achieves a specific capacitance (40.4 mA h g−1 at 1 A g−1) calculated via discharging time in GCD profiles (Fig. 7d). The capacity value only decreases to 71.1% (28.7 mA h g−1) at 10 A g−1, indicating the good rate capability (Fig. 7e). As demonstrated in Fig. 7f, the electrode dynamics and internal resistances of the device are evaluated by Nyquist plots. R s (1.24 Ω) and R ct (2.1) Ω calculated from the equivalent circuit reveal a low intrinsic resistance and the fast reaction kinetics of the device. The Ragone plot of the device is shown in Fig. 7g, which exhibits a high energy density of 32.3 W h kg−1 at the power density of 800 W kg−1, and remains 22.9 W h kg−1 at a high power density of 7200 W kg−1, superior to those of previously reported asymmetric supercapacitors based on SnO2/carbon composites [46,54–57]. As a flexible supercapacitor, the flexibility and stability of the device are important in its practical application. Fig. 7h displays the CV curves of the device under different bending states from 0° to 180° at 20 mV s−1. There is no distinct change under bending angles, showing excellent flexibility and electrochemical stability of the device. Fig. 7i shows the cycling stability of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF//GQD/CNF device at 5 A g−1. The capacity of the device can maintain 86.1% of the original capacity after 5000 cycles, while the coulombic efficiency is stable at 101.9%. The excellent cyclic stability and coulombic efficiency of the device can be ascribed to the high stability and the good contact between electrode and gel electrolyte, resulting from abundant Sn–O–C covalent bonds between SnO2 and carbon materials.

Based on the above analytical results, the excellent energy storage performance of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF can be attributed to its multicomponent cooperative structure as illustrated in Fig. 8 , specifically as the following: i) Carbon nanofibers embedded with graphene quantum dots (GQD/CNF) as a multidimensional carbon skeleton improve the conductivity and buffer volume expansion of SnO2; ii) There are abundant Sn–O–C covalent bonds to enhance the interfacial interaction between SnO2 and carbon materials, which anchors SnO2 tightly into GQD/CNF carbon skeleton to be a stable integration; iii) SnO2 nanodots are uniformly distributed and contribute their high capacitance activity; iv) Sn nanodots reduce the intrinsic resistance, and provide good electron conductivity to enhance the pseudo capacitance behavior of SnO2; v) GQDs increase the conductivity, stability and mechanical strength of the electrode material. Moreover, it's worth noting that the addition of GQDs facilitates the formation of more Sn–O–C covalent bonds (from 40% to 56%), which is conducive to high rate performance with fast electrochemical reaction kinetics.

In summary, we have demonstrated the rational design of a novel carbon skeleton of carbon nanofibers embedded with graphene quantum dots to anchor Sn/SnO2 nanodots as a stable integration through a simple electrospinning and carbonization reduction process. GQD/CNF as a multidimensional carbon skeleton effectively improves the conductivity and buffers volume expansion of SnO2. More significantly, there are abundant Sn–O–C covalent bonds to enhance the interfacial interaction between SnO2 and carbon materials and facilitate the electron/ion transfer kinetics. As a self-supporting and binder-free positive electrode material, Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF exhibits a high specific capacity of 168.6 mAh g−1 (1349 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate capability. Moreover, the assembled flexible asymmetric supercapacitor Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF//GQD/CNF achieves a maximum energy density of 32.3 W h kg−1 and a maximum power density of 7200 W kg−1, and remarkable mechanical stability and cyclic stability. These promising electrochemical performances indicate that the reasonable design of Sn/SnO2/GQD/CNF can provide an effective way to construct a new flexible electrode material to meet the ever-growing demand for wearable fabrics.

Jingting Bu and Long Zhang: Performing the characterizations of the material. Lingli Cheng and Minghong Wu: Discussing the results and analyzing the data.
